sentences.daily: There is a fan in the room.
sentences.office: Is there a meeting room on this floor?
sentences.interview: There is a small room for interviews.
sentences.business: There is a conference room on the third floor.
sentences.formal: There is a vacant room available.
sentences.informal: Is there room for one more?

words.usageNote: Use 'room' for physical space or general space (uncountable).

sentences.daily: There is a large hall in our school.
sentences.office: There is an event hall on the ground floor.
sentences.interview: The interview was held in the main hall.
sentences.business: There is a seminar hall available for booking.
sentences.formal: There is a hall designated for public functions.
sentences.informal: Is there a hall big enough for 200 people?

words.usageNote: 'Hall' can be an entrance hall or a large event space.

words.simpleMeaning: a long passage inside a building

sentences.daily: There is a long corridor in our apartment.
sentences.office: There are CCTV cameras in the corridor.
sentences.interview: Wait in the corridor before your interview.
sentences.business: There is a notice board in the corridor.
sentences.formal: There is a fire exit at the end of the corridor.
sentences.informal: Someone is waiting in the corridor for you.

words.usageNote: 'Corridor' is the passage connecting rooms in a building.

words.simpleMeaning: the entrance area of a building

sentences.daily: There is a sofa in the hotel lobby.
sentences.office: Meet me in the office lobby.
sentences.interview: There is a receptionist in the lobby.
sentences.business: There is a check-in counter in the lobby.
sentences.formal: There is a visitor registration desk in the lobby.
sentences.informal: I'll wait for you in the lobby.

words.usageNote: 'Lobby' is typically the waiting/entrance area of a hotel or building.

words.simpleMeaning: the front desk area of a building

sentences.daily: Ask at the reception for the room key.
sentences.office: There is a receptionist at reception.
sentences.interview: Please wait at the reception.
sentences.business: There is a visitor log at reception.
sentences.formal: There is a security check at the reception.
sentences.informal: Is there anyone at the reception?

words.usageNote: Use 'reception' for front desks in hotels, offices, hospitals.

words.simpleMeaning: a private small room or office

sentences.daily: The manager is in his cabin.
sentences.office: There is an AC in the cabin.
sentences.interview: The interview will be held in Cabin 3.
sentences.business: There is a lock on the cabin door.
sentences.formal: There is a nameplate outside each cabin.
sentences.informal: Is the boss in his cabin?

words.usageNote: In Indian English, 'cabin' often means a manager's private office.

sentences.daily: Which department do you work in?
sentences.office: There is a new policy in our department.
sentences.interview: There are five departments in this company.
sentences.business: There is an urgent meeting in the HR department.
sentences.formal: There is a head of each department.
sentences.informal: Is there anyone from the IT department here?

words.usageNote: 'Department' is a division of an office, school, or government.

words.simpleMeaning: a level of a building, or the ground surface

sentences.daily: Our office is on the third floor.
sentences.office: Is there a cafeteria on this floor?
sentences.interview: The HR office is on the second floor.
sentences.business: There are eight floors in this building.
sentences.formal: There is a fire exit on every floor.
sentences.informal: Which floor is the canteen on?

words.usageNote: 'Floor' = both the level of a building and the surface you walk on.

words.simpleMeaning: a structure with walls and a roof

sentences.daily: There is a new building near our school.
sentences.office: Is there a parking lot in this building?
sentences.interview: The company is in the old building.
sentences.business: There are ten floors in the new building.
sentences.formal: There is a fire safety system in the building.
sentences.informal: Which building is the canteen in?

words.simpleMeaning: the grounds and buildings of a college/university

sentences.daily: There is a gym on our college campus.
sentences.office: Is there a cafeteria on the campus?
sentences.interview: The campus interview will be held tomorrow.
sentences.business: There is a new tech park campus opening soon.
sentences.formal: There is a security gate at the campus entrance.
sentences.informal: Is there good food on campus?

words.usageNote: 'Campus' usually refers to a school/college/company premises.

sentences.daily: There is a garden outside our house.
sentences.office: There is a beautiful garden in our office campus.
sentences.interview: The garden area is near the conference hall.
sentences.business: There is a garden view from the meeting room.
sentences.formal: There is a well-maintained garden in the premises.
sentences.informal: Is there a garden nearby for a walk?

words.usageNote: 'Garden' is a cultivated outdoor space; 'park' is usually public.

sentences.daily: There is a park near my house.
sentences.office: Is there a parking area in the office?
sentences.interview: Meet me at the park next to the office.
sentences.business: There is a business park in the suburb.
sentences.formal: There is a children's park in the residential area.
sentences.informal: Is there a park where I can jog?

words.usageNote: 'Park' = public outdoor space; also used for 'parking'.

words.simpleMeaning: an area for children to play

sentences.daily: There is a playground in our colony.
sentences.office: There is a playground near the office for kids.
sentences.interview: The school has a big playground.
sentences.business: A good playground is important for employee families.
sentences.formal: There is a well-equipped playground in the school.
sentences.informal: Is there a playground nearby?

words.usageNote: 'Playground' is specifically for playing, often for children.

sentences.daily: There is a hospital near my home.
sentences.office: Is there a hospital near the office?
sentences.interview: I have an interview at the hospital.
sentences.business: There is a corporate hospital in the city.
sentences.formal: There is a government hospital in the district.
sentences.informal: Is there a good hospital around here?

words.usageNote: 'Hospital' is for serious medical care; 'clinic' is for smaller issues.

sentences.daily: There is a clinic at the corner of our street.
sentences.office: Is there a clinic near the office?
sentences.interview: There is a health clinic in the company campus.
sentences.business: There is a dental clinic in the mall.
sentences.formal: There is a primary health clinic in every village.
sentences.informal: Is there a clinic open nearby?

words.usageNote: 'Clinic' is smaller than a hospital, usually for outpatient care.

sentences.daily: Is there a pharmacy near here?
sentences.office: There is a pharmacy in the hospital building.
sentences.interview: There is a pharmacy next to the clinic.
sentences.business: There is a 24-hour pharmacy in the mall.
sentences.formal: There is a licensed pharmacy on every hospital campus.
sentences.informal: Is there a pharmacy open at this hour?

words.usageNote: In Indian English, also called 'medical store' or 'chemist'.

sentences.daily: There is a school near my house.
sentences.office: Is there a school nearby for my kids?
sentences.interview: I studied at a government school.
sentences.business: There is a school sponsored by our company.
sentences.formal: There is a government school in every village.
sentences.informal: Is there a good school in this area?

sentences.daily: There is a good college in our city.
sentences.office: Is there a college near the office area?
sentences.interview: I completed my degree from this college.
sentences.business: There is a management college opening soon.
sentences.formal: There is a government college in the district.
sentences.informal: Is there a college nearby?

words.simpleMeaning: a place with books to read or borrow

sentences.daily: There is a library in our school.
sentences.office: Is there a digital library on the campus?
sentences.interview: I often study in the library.
sentences.business: There is a reference library for employees.
sentences.formal: There is a well-stocked library in the institution.
sentences.informal: Is there a library around here?

words.usageNote: 'Library' has books you can borrow; 'bookshop' is where you buy them.

words.simpleMeaning: a place inside a building to eat

sentences.daily: There is a canteen in our school.
sentences.office: Is there a canteen on this floor?
sentences.interview: We had lunch in the office canteen.
sentences.business: There is a well-equipped canteen in the factory.
sentences.formal: There is a subsidized canteen for employees.
sentences.informal: Is the canteen open now?

words.usageNote: 'Canteen' is inside a school/office/factory; 'restaurant' is public.

sentences.daily: There is a cafeteria in our college.
sentences.office: Is there a cafeteria with healthy food?
sentences.interview: Let's talk in the cafeteria.
sentences.business: There is a modern cafeteria in our office.
sentences.formal: There is a cafeteria available for all employees.
sentences.informal: Is there good coffee in the cafeteria?

words.simpleMeaning: a place where you can buy and eat food

sentences.daily: There is a good restaurant near my home.
sentences.office: Is there a restaurant within walking distance?
sentences.interview: Let's meet at the restaurant for a discussion.
sentences.business: There is a restaurant on the ground floor of the hotel.
sentences.formal: There is a fine dining restaurant on the top floor.
sentences.informal: Is there a good restaurant around here?

words.usageNote: 'Restaurant' is a public eating place; you pay for each meal.

words.simpleMeaning: a building where people stay for a short time

sentences.daily: There is a hotel near the railway station.
sentences.office: Is there a hotel room available?
sentences.interview: The meeting is at the hotel lobby.
sentences.business: There is a 5-star hotel in the city centre.
sentences.formal: There is a hotel with conference facilities.
sentences.informal: Is there a budget hotel nearby?

words.usageNote: 'Hotel' offers rooms; 'restaurant' offers food (may be inside a hotel).

sentences.daily: There is a new mall in our city.
sentences.office: Is there a mall near the office?
sentences.interview: The company office is inside the mall.
sentences.business: There is a food court in the mall.
sentences.formal: There is a mall that houses 200 shops.
sentences.informal: Is there anything interesting in the mall?

words.usageNote: 'Mall' = large, enclosed shopping complex with many stores.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where goods are bought and sold

sentences.daily: There is a vegetable market near our home.
sentences.office: Is there a fruit market nearby?
sentences.interview: I come from a business market background.
sentences.business: There is a growing market for tech products.
sentences.formal: There is a regulated market for agricultural produce.
sentences.informal: Is the market open on Sundays?

words.usageNote: 'Market' can be physical or financial (stock market).

sentences.daily: There is a weekly bazaar in our village.
sentences.office: Is there a bazaar near the office?
sentences.interview: I grew up near a famous bazaar.
sentences.business: There is a big bazaar for wholesale goods.
sentences.formal: There is a traditional bazaar in the old city.
sentences.informal: Let's go to the bazaar this evening.

words.usageNote: 'Bazaar' = traditional open-air market, often used in South Asian context.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where goods are sold

sentences.daily: There is a sweet shop near my home.
sentences.office: Is there a stationery shop nearby?
sentences.interview: My family runs a clothing shop.
sentences.business: There is a new mobile shop in the market.
sentences.formal: There is a licensed shop for medicines.
sentences.informal: Is there a shop open at this hour?

words.usageNote: 'Shop' (British) = 'store' (American); both refer to retail selling places.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where things are kept or sold

sentences.daily: There is a grocery store near my house.
sentences.office: Is there a stationary store nearby?
sentences.interview: I worked at a retail store before.
sentences.business: There is a large chain store in the city.
sentences.formal: There is a storage room in the facility.
sentences.informal: Is there a store where I can buy snacks?

words.usageNote: 'Store' (American) = shop; also means a place to keep goods.

sentences.daily: There is a bank near our office.
sentences.office: Is there a bank branch nearby?
sentences.interview: I have an interview at the bank tomorrow.
sentences.business: There is a bank for all corporate transactions.
sentences.formal: There is a nationalised bank in every district.
sentences.informal: Is there a bank open today?

words.usageNote: 'Bank' handles money — savings, loans, transfers etc.

sentences.daily: Is there an ATM nearby?
sentences.office: There is an ATM in the office lobby.
sentences.interview: Is there an ATM near the interview venue?
sentences.business: There is an ATM inside the mall.
sentences.formal: There is an ATM available 24 hours.
sentences.informal: Is there an ATM around? I need cash.

words.usageNote: 'ATM' = Automated Teller Machine; used for cash withdrawal.

words.simpleMeaning: a place for sending mail and packages

sentences.daily: There is a post office in our locality.
sentences.office: Is there a post office near the office?
sentences.interview: I sent my documents through the post office.
sentences.business: There is a speed post service at the post office.
sentences.formal: There is a head post office in each district.
sentences.informal: Is the post office open on Sundays?

words.usageNote: 'Post office' handles letters, parcels, and money orders.

sentences.daily: There is a railway station near our house.
sentences.office: Is there a railway station within walking distance?
sentences.interview: Meet me at the railway station.
sentences.business: There is a hotel near the railway station.
sentences.formal: There is a large railway station in the city.
sentences.informal: Which way is the railway station?

words.usageNote: 'Railway station' = train station; 'bus stand' = for buses.

sentences.daily: There is a bus stop near my house.
sentences.office: Is there a bus stop near the office?
sentences.interview: Wait for me at the bus stop.
sentences.business: There is a bus stop in front of the building.
sentences.formal: There is a designated bus stop at every 500 metres.
sentences.informal: Is there a bus stop nearby?

words.usageNote: 'Bus stop' = regular stop; 'bus stand' = main station for buses.

words.simpleMeaning: a place for aeroplanes to land and take off

sentences.daily: There is an airport in our city.
sentences.office: Is there a pickup service to the airport?
sentences.interview: The company office is near the airport.
sentences.business: There is an international airport in the city.
sentences.formal: There is an airport with modern facilities.
sentences.informal: Is there an airport near here?

words.usageNote: 'Airport' is for aeroplanes; 'railway station' for trains.

sentences.daily: Is there a metro station near here?
sentences.office: There is a metro station in front of our office.
sentences.interview: Take the metro to reach the interview venue.
sentences.business: There is a metro station connecting the business district.
sentences.formal: There is a metro station at every major junction.
sentences.informal: Is there a metro station nearby?

sentences.daily: There is a temple near my house.
sentences.office: Is there a temple in this area?
sentences.interview: I take blessings at the temple before an interview.
sentences.business: There is a temple in our business complex.
sentences.formal: There is a heritage temple in the old city.
sentences.informal: Is there a temple nearby?

sentences.daily: There is a church near the market.
sentences.office: Is there a church in this area?
sentences.interview: The meeting is near the old church.
sentences.business: There is a heritage church in the town.
sentences.formal: There is a church that dates back 200 years.
sentences.informal: Is there a church nearby?

sentences.daily: There is a mosque at the end of the street.
sentences.office: Is there a mosque near the office?
sentences.interview: The interview venue is near the mosque.
sentences.business: There is a mosque in the old city.
sentences.formal: There is a grand mosque in the town centre.
sentences.informal: Is there a mosque in this area?

words.usageNote: 'Mosque' = Muslim place of worship; also called 'masjid'.

sentences.daily: There is a shrine in our village.
sentences.office: Is there a famous shrine nearby?
sentences.interview: The city has a famous shrine.
sentences.business: There is a shrine attracting thousands of tourists.
sentences.formal: There is a protected shrine in the heritage zone.
sentences.informal: Is there a shrine to visit here?

words.usageNote: 'Shrine' = a sacred place or small worship area.

sentences.daily: There is a stadium in our city.
sentences.office: Is there a stadium near the office?
sentences.interview: The stadium hosts many events.
sentences.business: There is a cricket stadium in the city.
sentences.formal: There is a national stadium for sporting events.
sentences.informal: Is there a stadium nearby?

sentences.daily: There is an auditorium in our school.
sentences.office: Is there an auditorium in the building?
sentences.interview: The presentation is in the auditorium.
sentences.business: There is an auditorium for 500 people.
sentences.formal: There is an auditorium for cultural events.
sentences.informal: Is there an auditorium available for our event?

words.usageNote: 'Auditorium' = large hall for performances or lectures.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where films are shown

sentences.daily: There is a cinema near my house.
sentences.office: Is there a cinema in the mall?
sentences.interview: I worked at a cinema hall before.
sentences.business: There is a multiplex cinema opening soon.
sentences.formal: There is a cinema complex in the cultural centre.
sentences.informal: Is there a good cinema nearby?

words.usageNote: 'Cinema' = movie theatre; 'multiplex' = cinema with multiple screens.

sentences.daily: There is a theatre in our city.
sentences.office: Is there a theatre club in the office?
sentences.interview: I have experience in theatre.
sentences.business: There is a corporate theatre event tonight.
sentences.formal: There is a renowned theatre in the cultural district.
sentences.informal: Is there a theatre show this weekend?

words.simpleMeaning: a place where historical items are kept

sentences.daily: There is a museum in our city.
sentences.office: Is there a museum near the office?
sentences.interview: I worked as a guide at the museum.
sentences.business: There is a science museum in the tech park.
sentences.formal: There is a national museum in the capital.
sentences.informal: Is there a museum worth visiting here?

words.usageNote: 'Museum' = displays historical, scientific, or artistic items.

words.simpleMeaning: a room or building for displaying art

sentences.daily: There is an art gallery near our college.
sentences.office: Is there a gallery in the building?
sentences.interview: I interned at an art gallery.
sentences.business: There is a gallery for displaying company achievements.
sentences.formal: There is a gallery showcasing local art.
sentences.informal: Is there a gallery nearby?

words.usageNote: 'Gallery' = display of art; also used for shopping areas.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where legal cases are heard

sentences.daily: There is a court near our office.
sentences.office: Is there a court nearby?
sentences.interview: I have a court appearance tomorrow.
sentences.business: There is a commercial court in the city.
sentences.formal: There is a high court in the state capital.
sentences.informal: Is there a court in this area?

words.usageNote: 'Court' = legal court; also a 'sports court' (tennis court).

words.simpleMeaning: a place where police work from

sentences.daily: There is a police station in our locality.
sentences.office: Is there a police station near the office?
sentences.interview: I have an interview at the police station.
sentences.business: There is a police station near the market.
sentences.formal: There is a police station in every locality.
sentences.informal: Is there a police station nearby?

words.usageNote: 'Police station' = where police officers are based.

words.simpleMeaning: the highest lawmaking body of a country

sentences.daily: There is a parliament in our capital.
sentences.office: Is there a parliamentary session today?
sentences.interview: I visited the parliament on a school trip.
sentences.business: There is a new bill in parliament.
sentences.formal: There is a parliament with two houses.
sentences.informal: Is there a parliament building nearby to visit?

words.simpleMeaning: the official office of a country in another country

sentences.daily: There is an Indian embassy in London.
sentences.office: Is there an embassy near the office area?
sentences.interview: I have a visa interview at the embassy.
sentences.business: There is an embassy for trade relations.
sentences.formal: There is an embassy representing each country.
sentences.informal: Is there an embassy in this city?

words.simpleMeaning: a smaller diplomatic office in another city

sentences.daily: There is a consulate in the city.
sentences.office: Is there a consulate nearby for a visa?
sentences.interview: I need to visit the consulate for documents.
sentences.business: There is a consulate for trade-related services.
sentences.formal: There is a consulate in the major cities.
sentences.informal: Is there a consulate here?

words.usageNote: 'Consulate' is a smaller diplomatic office, often for visa services.

sentences.daily: There are a few apples in the basket.
sentences.office: There are a few issues to resolve.
sentences.interview: There are a few factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are a few aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is a few evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there a few left?

words.usageNote: 'a few' = a small positive number; used with countable nouns.

words.simpleMeaning: more than two but not very many

sentences.daily: There are several people waiting outside.
sentences.office: There are several tasks pending.
sentences.interview: There are several factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are several aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is several evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there several left?

words.usageNote: 'Several' = more than two, usually 3-7; countable nouns.

words.simpleMeaning: a large number of people or things

sentences.daily: There are many shops in the market.
sentences.office: There are many pending reports.
sentences.interview: There are many factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are many aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is many evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there many left?

words.usageNote: 'Many' = with countable plural nouns; questions and negatives.

words.simpleMeaning: a large amount of something uncountable

sentences.daily: There is not much time left.
sentences.office: There is too much work today.
sentences.interview: There are much factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are much aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is much evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there much left?

words.usageNote: 'Much' = with uncountable nouns; usually in negatives/questions.

sentences.daily: There is some milk in the fridge.
sentences.office: There are some files on the table.
sentences.interview: There are some factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are some aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is some evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there some left?

sentences.daily: Is there any water left?
sentences.office: Are there any updates?
sentences.interview: There are any factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are any aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is any evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there any left?

sentences.daily: There is no water in the glass.
sentences.office: There are no pending tasks.
sentences.interview: There are no factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are no aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is no evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there no left?

words.usageNote: 'No' before a noun = strong negative; 'not any' = same meaning.

sentences.daily: None of the shops are open.
sentences.office: None of the emails were replied.
sentences.interview: There are none factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are none aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is none evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there none left?

words.usageNote: 'None' is a pronoun, used alone; 'no' is a determiner.

words.simpleMeaning: a large amount, more than enough

sentences.daily: There is plenty of food at the party.
sentences.office: There are plenty of resources available.
sentences.interview: There are plenty of factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are plenty of aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is plenty of evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there plenty of left?

words.usageNote: 'Plenty of' = more than enough; both countable and uncountable.

sentences.daily: There is a lot of traffic today.
sentences.office: There are a lot of pending emails.
sentences.interview: There are a lot of factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are a lot of aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is a lot of evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there a lot of left?

words.usageNote: 'A lot of' = both countable and uncountable; informal.

sentences.daily: There are lots of birds in the tree.
sentences.office: There are lots of tasks to finish.
sentences.interview: There are lots of factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are lots of aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is lots of evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there lots of left?

words.usageNote: 'Lots of' = informal form of 'a lot of'.

sentences.daily: There is enough milk for everyone.
sentences.office: Are there enough chairs in the room?
sentences.interview: There are enough factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are enough aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is enough evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there enough left?

words.usageNote: 'Enough' = sufficient; can go before or after adjective.

sentences.daily: There are too many people in the room.
sentences.office: There are too many meetings today.
sentences.interview: There are too many factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are too many aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is too many evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there too many left?

words.usageNote: 'Too many' = with countable nouns; negative meaning.

sentences.daily: There is too much noise outside.
sentences.office: There is too much workload this month.
sentences.interview: There are too much factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are too much aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is too much evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there too much left?

words.usageNote: 'Too much' = with uncountable nouns; negative meaning.

words.simpleMeaning: not many at all; a small number

sentences.daily: There are very few options available.
sentences.office: There are very few staff today.
sentences.interview: There are very few factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are very few aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is very few evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there very few left?

words.usageNote: 'Very few' = emphasizes smallness; with countable nouns.

sentences.daily: There is very little water in the tank.
sentences.office: There is very little time left.
sentences.interview: There are very little factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are very little aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is very little evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there very little left?

words.usageNote: 'Very little' = with uncountable nouns; emphasizes smallness.

words.simpleMeaning: many groups of twelve; a large number

sentences.daily: There are dozens of birds in the garden.
sentences.office: There are dozens of pending requests.
sentences.interview: There are dozens of factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are dozens of aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is dozens of evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there dozens of left?

sentences.daily: There are hundreds of shops in the market.
sentences.office: There are hundreds of employees in the company.
sentences.interview: There are hundreds of factors to consider.
sentences.business: There are hundreds of aspects in business.
sentences.formal: There is hundreds of evidence to support this.
sentences.informal: Is there hundreds of left?

sentences.daily: There are thousands of books in the library.
sentences.office: There are thousands of customers.

sentences.daily: Does a solution really exist?
sentences.office: The policy exists in writing.
sentences.interview: A better opportunity exists.
sentences.business: A market for this exists.
sentences.formal: The problem exists at all levels.
sentences.informal: Does that place even exist?

words.simpleMeaning: to come into view or exist

sentences.daily: A solution will appear soon.
sentences.office: An error appeared in the system.
sentences.interview: The opportunity appeared at the right time.
sentences.business: A trend has appeared in the market.
sentences.formal: A discrepancy appeared in the accounts.
sentences.informal: A problem just appeared!

words.usageNote: 'Appear' = become visible or start to exist.

sentences.daily: An accident occurred on the road.
sentences.office: An error occurred in the system.
sentences.interview: Problems occur in every job.
sentences.business: A crisis occurred in the market.
sentences.formal: An incident occurred yesterday.
sentences.informal: Something occurred at the market.

words.simpleMeaning: to continue to be; to stay

sentences.office: There remain many issues to resolve.
sentences.interview: There remains scope for improvement.
sentences.business: There remain some challenges.
sentences.formal: There remains a need for action.
sentences.informal: There's still some work remaining.

words.simpleMeaning: to come up; to appear suddenly

sentences.daily: A problem arose during the trip.
sentences.office: An issue arose in the meeting.
sentences.interview: Questions may arise during the interview.
sentences.business: A dispute arose over the contract.
sentences.formal: A concern has arisen.
sentences.informal: A problem just arose!

words.simpleMeaning: to come out and be seen

sentences.daily: A solution has emerged.
sentences.office: A leader emerged from the team.
sentences.interview: New skills emerge with practice.
sentences.business: A new market has emerged.
sentences.formal: A consensus has emerged.
sentences.informal: Something new has emerged.

words.usageNote: 'Emerge' = come out from hiding or obscurity.

words.simpleMeaning: to come about as a result

sentences.daily: There will be results from hard work.
sentences.office: There resulted a delay in the process.
sentences.interview: Success results from effort.
sentences.business: Growth results from investment.
sentences.formal: A complication resulted.
sentences.informal: What has resulted from all this?

words.usageNote: 'Result in' = cause; 'result from' = be caused by.
